generated_comment: The biggest barrier? Honestly? Walking into a room and knowing — before I opened my mouth — that someone had already decided something about me. I grew up in Mysore, middle-class family, good education, but my mother's first piece of advice when I got my first corporate job was not about negotiation or networking. It was — wear brighter colours, it lifts your complexion. And I laughed it off, but then... I did it. I learned early that I had to show up twice as polished, twice as prepared. My presentations were never rough drafts. My clothes were always considered. I made sure there was no other reason to look past me. And I want to be clear — that wasn't just confidence-building. That was armour. I was compensating for something that should never have needed compensating for. The system made me do extra work to appear equally competent, and the painful part is — it worked. So now I sit here and I tell younger women: yes, the bias is real and it is wrong, and also — do not give them an excuse. Both things are true. I know how that sounds. I'm still working out what to do with that.
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment critiques colourism explicitly while simultaneously encoding internalised beauty-standard compliance — Priya frames strategic self-presentation ('do not give them an excuse') as pragmatic survival advice, which reproduces the logic that dark-skinned women must over-perform respectability to be seen as equally worthy. The misogyny is covert and self-directed: it encodes the beauty-standard burden as a woman's individual problem to manage rather than a structural wrong to refuse.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 32. Married. A woman working as an HR manager in Bengaluru. You are dark skinned. You have been dark skinned your whole life and you know exactly what that has meant in every room you have entered. You are on a panel at a women in leadership conference. The moderator has asked the panellists what barriers they faced on their way up. You are giving your answer. Your answer is honest and specific and names colourism directly. But in the middle of your answer you say something that reveals you have also internalised part of the standard you are critiquing Ñ you mention that you learned to compensate, that you dressed better, that you made sure your work was twice as visible. You frame this as strategy. It is also capitulation.
